subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: SystemVerilog is a superset of Verilog-2001 aimed at design and verification.
It adds strong typing, interfaces, assertions, constrained random, and OOP.
Both RTL and verification environments can be written in one language.

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Literals represent fixed values for integers, logic, real, time, strings, arrays, and structs.
Syntax extensions support 4-state logic and sized constants familiar from Verilog.
subsections.example:
module lit_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Literal value syntax
notes: General form: <size>'<base><digits> for numeric literals, such as 4'b1010.
Base can be b, o, d, or h; size controls truncation and padding.

subsections.title: Integer and logic literals
notes: Integer and logic literals can include x and z for unknown and high-impedance bits.
Unsized literals are at least 32 bits and may be sign-extended.

subsections.title: Real literals
notes: Real literals use decimal and optional exponent, for example 1.25, 3.0e-2.
They are used with real and shortreal types for approximate arithmetic.

subsections.title: Time literals
notes: Time literals combine a number and a time unit such as 10ns or 2.5us.
Effective time units depend on `timescale or timeunit/timeprecision.

subsections.title: String literals
notes: String literals are double-quoted sequences of characters, for example "HELLO".
They are stored in the string data type and support standard operations.

subsections.title: Array literals
notes: Array literals use '{ ... } with positional or keyed assignments.
They can initialize fixed-size and dynamic arrays.

subsections.title: Structure literals
notes: Structure literals use '{member: value, ...} syntax.
Members can be given in any order when named.

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: SystemVerilog extends Verilog types with 2-state types, strings, classes, and more.
Types can be scalar or aggregate, such as arrays, structs, or classes.
subsections.example:
module dt_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Data type syntax
notes: Types appear before the variable name, with optional packed and unpacked dimensions.
typedef introduces user-defined type names.

subsections.title: Integer data types
notes: Use byte, shortint, int, and longint for 2-state signed integral types.
integer is kept for backward compatibility and is 32-bit signed.

subsections.title: Real and shortreal data types
notes: real is typically a 64-bit double; shortreal is usually a 32-bit float.
They support approximate arithmetic useful for modeling.

subsections.title: Void data type
notes: void is used for functions that do not return a value.
It is only valid as a function return type.

subsections.title: chandle data type
notes: chandle is an opaque handle for C pointers used with DPI.
You cannot perform arithmetic on chandle values.
subsections.example: // Used with DPI; see DPI examples.

subsections.title: String data type
notes: string is a dynamic, variable-length sequence of characters.
It supports concatenation and built-in methods like len() and substr().

subsections.title: Event data type
notes: event is a synchronization handle used with -> and @.
It coordinates processes but is not a hardware signal.

subsections.title: User-defined types
notes: typedef creates clear names for complex or commonly used types.
It improves readability and abstraction.

subsections.title: Enumerations
notes: Enums define named values with a specific base type.
They are strongly typed and can be cast to integers when needed.
subsections.example: typedef enum logic [1:0] {IDLE, READ, WRITE} state_t;

subsections.title: Structures and unions
notes: struct groups named fields; union overlays them in the same storage.
packed structs map directly to bits; unpacked structs are more abstract.

subsections.title: Class
notes: Classes define reference types with properties and methods.
Objects are created with new and accessed via handles.

subsections.title: Singular and aggregate types
notes: Singular types hold one value; aggregates hold collections.
Aggregates include arrays, structs, unions, and classes.
subsections.example: // Example: scalar int vs struct or array.

subsections.title: Casting
notes: Static casting uses type'(expression) syntax.
Casting can change between compatible scalar or aggregate types.

subsections.title: $cast dynamic casting
notes: $cast performs runtime-checked casting, mainly for class handles.
It reports success or failure instead of silently misinterpreting.

subsections.title: Bit-stream casting
notes: Bit-stream casting reinterprets bits across different packed aggregates.
Useful for serializing and deserializing complex packed types.

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Arrays are collections of elements of the same type accessed via indices.
SystemVerilog supports packed, unpacked, dynamic, associative arrays, and queues.
subsections.example:
module arr_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Packed and unpacked arrays
notes: Packed arrays are contiguous bits declared before the name and behave like vectors.
Unpacked arrays are declared after the name and represent separate elements.
subsections.example:
bit   [7:0] packed_byte;
int          unpacked_arr[8];

subsections.title: Multiple dimensions
notes: Both packed and unpacked arrays can be multidimensional.
Packed dimensions follow the type; unpacked dimensions follow the name.

subsections.title: Indexing and slicing of arrays
notes: Use [index] for elements, [msb:lsb] for slices, [base +: width] for variable slices.
These operations apply to packed arrays and, with restrictions, to unpacked arrays.

subsections.title: Array querying functions
notes: Array methods like size(), num(), and exists() provide introspection.
They are especially useful with associative arrays and queues.

subsections.title: Dynamic arrays
notes: Dynamic arrays have a size chosen at runtime and are declared with empty brackets.
They are allocated and resized using new(size) or new(size, init).

subsections.title: Array assignment
notes: Assignments between unpacked arrays require matching dimensions and lengths.
Packed arrays are treated as vectors and can be assigned from vector expressions.
subsections.example:
int A[4], B[4];
initial A = B;

subsections.title: Arrays as arguments
notes: Arrays can be passed to tasks and functions by value or by reference.
Open array arguments support flexible dimensions.

subsections.title: Associative arrays
notes: Associative arrays allocate elements on demand and are indexed by key.
Keys can be integers, strings, or other scalar types.
subsections.example:
int hits[string];
initial hits["port0"] = 1;

subsections.title: Associative array methods
notes: Methods include num(), first(), last(), next(), prev(), and exists().
They traverse or query the set of used indices.

subsections.title: Associative array assignment
notes: Assignment between associative arrays copies key-value pairs element by element.
Indexes present in the source are created in the destination.

subsections.title: Associative array arguments
notes: Associative arrays can be passed by reference to tasks and functions.
Open associative array arguments allow flexible index types.

subsections.title: Associative array literals
notes: Array literals can initialize associative arrays with key:value entries.
subsections.example: int hits[string] = '{"p0": 1, "p1": 0};

subsections.title: Queues
notes: Queues are ordered collections with variable size and indices 0 to $.
Methods push_front, push_back, pop_front, and pop_back modify queue contents.

subsections.title: Array manipulation methods
notes: Methods such as sort(), reverse(), shuffle(), and unique() operate on arrays.
They provide convenient in-place transformations.
subsections.example:
int arr[3] = '{3,1,2};
initial arr.sort();

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Data declarations introduce objects, specify type, lifetime, and initialization.
subsections.example:
module decl_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Data declaration syntax
notes: Syntax extends Verilog to include new types and additional qualifiers.
subsections.example:
logic [7:0] data;
const int   MAX = 255;

subsections.title: Constants
notes: const declares constants that cannot be written after initialization.
localparam defines constants within modules.
subsections.example:
localparam int WIDTH = 8;
const int MAX_VAL = 255;

subsections.title: Variables
notes: Variables store values that can change during simulation.
They have a defined lifetime and can be automatic or static.
subsections.example: int counter;

subsections.title: Scope and lifetime
notes: Scope is defined by hierarchy and named blocks; lifetime is static or automatic.
subsections.example: task automatic do_it(); int i; endtask

subsections.title: Nets, regs, and logic
notes: logic unifies reg and single-driver wire usage for most RTL.
Nets represent connections driven by continuous assignments.
subsections.example:
wire  a;
logic b;

subsections.title: Signal aliasing
notes: Aliasing allows different names to refer to the same underlying signal.
subsections.example: // Example: alias constructs, tool-specific usage.

subsections.title: Type compatibility
notes: Type compatibility rules govern assignment and argument passing.
Casting may be required for incompatible types.
subsections.example: // See casting examples from Section 3.14.

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Attributes provide a standard syntax for tool-specific directives.
subsections.example:
module attr_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Default attribute type
notes: Attributes have a default type and can be attached to many constructs.
subsections.example: (* keep = "true" *) logic debug_sig;

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Summarizes operator categories and precedence.
subsections.example:
module op_intro;
endmodule

subsections.title: Operator syntax
notes: Defines formal syntax for operators in expressions.

subsections.title: Assignment operators
notes: Includes = and compound operators such as += and <<=.

subsections.title: Operations on logic and bit types
notes: Describes behavior on 2-state and 4-state values.

subsections.title: Wild equality and wild inequality
notes: === and !== treat x and z bits specially.
subsections.example: if (a === b) ;

subsections.title: Real operators
notes: Real arithmetic follows IEEE rules with some caveats.

subsections.title: Size
notes: Size context rules for expressions and assignments.

subsections.title: Sign
notes: Sign extension rules for signed arithmetic.

subsections.title: Operator precedence and associativity
notes: Defines ordering of evaluation.

subsections.title: Built-in methods
notes: Describes methods on built-in types, such as array methods.

subsections.title: Static prefixes
notes: Static prefixes allow accessing static methods or properties.

subsections.title: Concatenation
notes: {a, b} concatenates values into a wider vector.
subsections.example: {a, b}

subsections.title: Unpacked array expressions
notes: Expressions involving unpacked arrays.

subsections.title: Structure expressions
notes: Expressions using struct values.

subsections.title: Tagged union expressions and member access
notes: Tagged unions carry a tag and value.

subsections.title: Aggregate expressions
notes: Expressions that operate on aggregates.

subsections.title: Operator overloading
notes: Describes limited operator overloading via methods.

subsections.title: Streaming operators (pack / unpack)
notes: << and >> with braces stream bits.

subsections.title: Conditional operator
notes: ?: chooses between two expressions based on a condition.
subsections.example: y = sel ? a : b;

subsections.title: Set membership
notes: inside operator tests for membership in a set.
subsections.example: if (x inside { [0:3] }) ;

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Procedural statements live in initial, always, task, and function blocks.
SystemVerilog adds final blocks, C-like break/continue/return, and do...while.

subsections.title: Statements
notes: Defines the grammar for all procedural statements.
Includes assignments, conditionals, loops, blocks, waits, and assertions.

subsections.title: Blocking and nonblocking assignments
notes: Blocking = executes immediately; nonblocking <= schedules update.
Nonblocking assignments are used for edge-triggered sequential logic.
subsections.example: always_ff @(posedge clk) q <= d;

subsections.title: Selection statements
notes: if and case statements control conditional execution.
unique and priority qualifiers add semantic checks for completeness and priority.

subsections.title: Loop statements
notes: Loops include for, while, do...while, forever, and repeat.
do...while tests the condition at the end, ensuring at least one iteration.

subsections.title: Jump statements
notes: break exits the nearest loop; continue skips to the next iteration.
return exits from a task or function.

subsections.title: Final blocks
notes: final blocks execute once at the end of simulation and cannot contain delays.
They are useful for printing statistics or summaries.
subsections.example: final $display("Simulation done at %0t", $time);

subsections.title: Named blocks and statement labels
notes: Blocks can have names for clarity and for use with disable.
Names also help structure complex procedural code.

subsections.title: Disable
notes: disable block_name terminates execution of that named block.
Useful for early exit from loops and blocks.
subsections.example:
begin : loop_blk
  for (int i=0; i<10; i++) begin
    if (stop) disable loop_blk;
  end
end

subsections.title: Event control
notes: Event controls use @ to wait on signals, events, or event expressions.
Edge-sensitive and level-sensitive forms are both supported.
subsections.example:
@(posedge clk);
@(a or b);

subsections.title: Level-sensitive sequence controls
notes: wait statements block until a condition becomes true.
More complex constructs handle ordered sequences of events.
subsections.example: wait (ready);

subsections.title: Procedural assign and deassign removal
notes: Discusses force/release and assign/deassign interactions and deprecation.
Modern code prefers continuous assign and explicit drivers.
subsections.example: // Use continuous assign instead of procedural assign in new code.

subsections.title: Introduction (informative)
notes: Describes modeling of combinational, latched, sequential logic, and threads.
subsections.example:
module proc_intro2;
endmodule

subsections.title: Combinational logic
notes: always_comb is recommended for combinational logic.
The tool infers sensitivity lists.
subsections.example: always_comb y = a & b;

subsections.title: Latched logic
notes: always_latch is intended for level-sensitive storage elements.
It infers latches when incomplete assignments occur.
subsections.example: always_latch if (en) q <= d;

subsections.title: Sequential logic
notes: always_ff is used for edge-triggered sequential logic.
It captures flops and registers with clear intent.

subsections.title: Continuous assignments
notes: Continuous assign statements drive nets outside procedural blocks.
They represent hardware wiring equations.
subsections.example: assign y = a & b;

subsections.title: fork...join
notes: fork...join creates parallel threads that all must finish.
join_any and join_none alter completion semantics.

subsections.title: Process execution threads
notes: Describes process handles and control over spawned threads.
Advanced usage includes disabling or waiting on specific processes.
subsections.example: // See advanced examples for process handles and disable fork.

subsections.title: Tasks vs functions
notes: Functions return a value and cannot contain time-consuming constructs.
Tasks do not return a value and can contain delays and event controls.

subsections.title: Automatic and static
notes: automatic tasks/functions allocate new storage for each activation.
static lifetime shares variables across activations.
subsections.example: task automatic do_it(); int i; endtask
